title: Label a House Key
summary: Label a key so you know which lock it opens without having to guess.
learnerPromise: You will be able to label any key clearly and safely so you can tell it apart from others at a glance.
whyItMatters: Most people carry several keys. Clear labels save time, prevent you from trying the wrong lock, and help you return a lost key to its owner more easily.
realLifeUses: Tell your front door key apart from your back door key in the dark
Label a spare key you keep in a drawer so you remember what it opens
Mark keys for a friend or family member who is watching your home while you are away
Organize a set of keys after moving into a new place
materialsNeeded: The key you want to label
A key tag, label sticker, or permanent marker
Optional: key ring or color-coded cover
result: A labeled key that you can identify by touch or sight without testing it in a lock.
youWillLearn: Choose a labeling method that works for your situation
Write or attach a label that stays readable over time
Avoid putting private information on a key that could be lost or stolen
Test that the label is secure and will not fall off with daily use
miniChallenge: Pick one unlabeled key you use regularly and label it with the name of the door or lock it opens.
steps: Test the key in its lock to confirm which door or item it opens
Choose a labeling method: key tag, color ring, engraved label, or sticker
Write a clear label using a name you will recognize, not just an address
Attach the label securely and check that it will not slip off
Put the key back on your keyring and confirm you can identify it by touch or sight
completionCriteria: Your key has a readable label that identifies what it opens
The label is attached well enough to stay on during normal use
commonProblems: Labels fade or peel off after being in a pocket for weeks
Writing the address on a key is risky if the key is lost
Some keys are too small for standard key tags
tips: Use 'front door' or 'garage' instead of your street address for safety
Color-coded rings let you tell keys apart even in low light
If a key is shared, use a generic label that the owner still understands
Check the label once a month and replace it if it is wearing out
